turns.content: Why do English auctions, Dutch auctions, and sealed-bid auctions produce different results? Doesn’t the highest bidder always win?

The highest-value bidder does not always win in equilibrium once you distinguish “value” from “bid” and account for strategic behavior, information, and risk. Different formats change (i) what information arrives during bidding, (ii) how costly it is to reveal information, (iii) the mapping from a bidder’s private information to an optimal bid, and (iv) what “price rule” is induced. A useful starting point is the independent private values (IPV) model: each bidder i has a private value v_i drawn i.i.d. from distribution F on [0, v̄], and knows only their own value. In that setting, with risk-neutral bidders and symmetric equilibrium, the Revenue Equivalence Theorem (RET) says that many “standard” auctions (English, Dutch, first-price sealed-bid, second-price sealed-bid) yield the same expected revenue and allocate the item to the highest value bidder. But the *path* and *bids* differ, and outside RET conditions (risk aversion, correlated/common values, asymmetries, reserve prices, entry, etc.), outcomes differ. Even under IPV, the “highest bidder wins” is trivially true as a mechanical rule: the highest *bid* wins. The point is that bidding strategies differ, so the highest-value person might shade more/less depending on format, and in some environments you can get misallocation. In common value settings, the winner’s curse changes things drastically, and the format’s information revelation changes bids and efficiency.
Can you formalize the three main formats and show how equilibrium bidding differs? Let’s assume risk-neutral IPV first.

Sure. Consider N ≥ 2 symmetric, risk-neutral bidders with values v ~ F with density f on [0, v̄]. Let V_(1) ≥ V_(2) ≥ ... denote order statistics (highest, second highest, etc.). 1) Second-price sealed-bid (Vickrey): each bidder submits bid b_i; highest bid wins, pays second-highest bid. - Dominant strategy: bid truthfully b(v) = v. - Allocation: highest v wins. - Expected payment of winner: E[V_(2)] conditional on being highest. 2) English (ascending) auction: price increases; bidders drop out when price exceeds their willingness to pay. Under IPV and private information, the standard model yields the same outcome as second-price: - In equilibrium, each bidder stays in until price reaches v_i, then drops. - Winner pays approximately the second-highest value (the dropout price of the runner-up). So English ≈ Vickrey under IPV (same allocation, same expected payments). 3) First-price sealed-bid: each submits b_i; highest bid wins, pays own bid. - In symmetric equilibrium, bidding function b(v) solves: b(v) = E[V_(1) of others | V_(1) < v] ??? More precisely: Let G be distribution of the maximum of N−1 other values: G(x) = F(x)^{N-1}, with density g(x) = (N−1)F(x)^{N−2}f(x). Bidder with value v who bids b wins if b > max_j≠i b(v_j). In monotone equilibrium, this is equivalent to v > max_j≠i v_j. Probability of winning is F(v)^{N−1}. Expected utility from bidding as if value is x: u(v,x) = (v − b(x)) F(x)^{N−1}. FOC at x=v gives: 0 = ∂u/∂x |_{x=v} = (v − b(v)) (N−1)F(v)^{N−2} f(v) − b'(v) F(v)^{N−1}. Rearrange: b'(v) = (v − b(v)) (N−1) f(v)/F(v). Solve ODE with boundary b(0)=0: b(v) = v − ∫_0^v [F(t)^{N−1}/F(v)^{N−1}] dt = v − (1/F(v)^{N−1}) ∫_0^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. This is strictly below v (bid shading). 4) Dutch (descending) auction: price starts high and ticks down; first bidder to accept wins at that price. Under IPV with risk neutrality and symmetric strategies, Dutch is strategically equivalent to first-price sealed-bid: choosing when to stop the clock is equivalent to choosing a bid. Thus the equilibrium “acceptance price” equals the first-price bid function b(v) above. So under IPV + risk neutrality + symmetry, English and second-price have truthful/“drop at value” behavior and pay second-highest value; Dutch and first-price have shading and pay a shaded amount. Yet expected revenues are equal by RET.
If expected revenues are equal, what “different results” remain? Isn’t the distribution of prices the same too?

Not necessarily. RET equates expected revenue (and expected utility of bidders) across a class of auctions with the same allocation rule and the same expected payment by the lowest type. But the *distribution* of revenue/prices can differ. - In second-price/English under IPV, the price equals V_(2) (second-highest value). So price distribution is that of the second order statistic. - In first-price/Dutch, price equals b(V_(1)) where V_(1) is the highest value transformed by the bid function. That distribution generally differs from V_(2), even though expectations match. So “different results” can mean: 1) Different bidding behavior (truthful vs shaded). 2) Different price distribution and variance. 3) Different information revealed during the process (English reveals dropout prices). 4) Different susceptibility to collusion, shill bidding, or entry dynamics. 5) Different outcomes when assumptions depart from IPV risk-neutral symmetry.
Can you show an explicit example with a distribution, say uniform[0,1], and compute the equilibrium bids and expected prices?

Take N bidders, v ~ U[0,1]. Then F(v)=v on [0,1]. First-price equilibrium bid: b(v) = v − (1/v^{N−1}) ∫_0^v t^{N−1} dt = v − (1/v^{N−1}) * (v^N / N) = v − v / N = (N−1)/N * v. So bid shading is linear. Expected price: Second-price/English: price = V_(2). For U[0,1], E[V_(k)] = k/(N+1) for the k-th order statistic from the bottom; from the top, E[V_(2)] (second highest) equals (N−1)/(N+1). (Because expected r-th highest equals (N−r+1)/(N+1).) So E[price]_2nd = (N−1)/(N+1). First-price/Dutch: price = b(V_(1)) = (N−1)/N * V_(1). And E[V_(1)] = N/(N+1). Thus E[price]_1st = (N−1)/N * N/(N+1) = (N−1)/(N+1), matching. But distributions differ: - Second price is V_(2), whose density differs from scaled V_(1). V_(1) has CDF x^N; V_(2) has a different CDF involving x^{N-1}(N - (N-1)x), etc.
Derive the distribution of V_(2) and compare to b(V_(1)) in the uniform case. I want the explicit CDFs.

For U[0,1], order statistics are tractable. Let Y = V_(1) = max of N i.i.d. uniforms. Then CDF: P(Y ≤ y) = y^N, y∈[0,1]. Density: f_Y(y) = N y^{N−1}. Let Z = V_(2) = second-highest. Its CDF: P(Z ≤ z) = P(at most one value exceeds z) = P(0 exceed z) + P(1 exceeds z) = z^N + N(1−z) z^{N−1} = z^{N−1}[z + N(1−z)] = z^{N−1}[N − (N−1)z]. Differentiate to get density: f_Z(z) = d/dz [z^N + N(1−z)z^{N−1}] = N z^{N−1} + N[ (−1) z^{N−1} + (1−z)(N−1) z^{N−2}] = N z^{N−1} − N z^{N−1} + N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2} = N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2}. Now compare W = b(Y) = ((N−1)/N) Y. Since Y∈[0,1], W∈[0,(N−1)/N]. CDF for w in that range: P(W ≤ w) = P(Y ≤ (N/(N−1)) w) = ((N/(N−1)) w)^N. So f_W(w) = N * (N/(N−1))^N * w^{N−1} on [0,(N−1)/N]. Z is supported on [0,1], W on [0,(N−1)/N]. They cannot have the same distribution; yet E[Z]=E[W]. In fact, second-price can yield prices close to 1 with positive probability (when top two values are high), while first-price price is capped below 1 in this uniform example because bids are shaded.
How does this connect to the idea that English auctions reveal information? Under IPV, do they reveal anything relevant?

Under IPV with independent private values, the information revealed by dropouts in an English auction doesn’t change anyone’s valuation, because valuations are independent and private. It can still affect beliefs about others’ values, but those beliefs are not payoff-relevant except through strategic behavior. And since the English auction has a dominant-strategy-like “stay until value” structure (more precisely, it is an ex post equilibrium under private values), information revelation doesn’t change optimal behavior. Under common values or affiliated signals, revelation matters because learning that others stay longer suggests the item is worth more, shifting your conditional expectation and hence your willingness to pay. Then auction format affects the winner’s curse and expected revenue (Milgrom-Weber linkage principle).
Let’s move to common value. Define a simple common-value model and compare first-price vs English outcomes.

A canonical common-value model: true value V is the same for all bidders but unknown. Each bidder i observes a signal s_i about V. Signals are affiliated (positively correlated). Each bidder’s expected value conditional on signals increases in their own signal and in others’ signals. In such settings: - Winning is bad news: it suggests your signal was the highest, so you likely overestimated V relative to others. This is the winner’s curse. - In sealed-bid first-price, you don’t observe others’ signals, so you must shade more to avoid the winner’s curse. - In English (ascending), the dropout points of others partially reveal their signals, letting the remaining bidders update upward/downward appropriately; this reduces the winner’s curse and tends to increase bids/revenue. Milgrom and Weber (1982) show that with affiliated signals and certain regularity conditions, expected revenue ranking often is: English (ascending) ≥ second-price ≥ first-price ≥ Dutch, and more generally, more information revelation increases expected revenue (linkage principle).
Can you illustrate with a tractable example, maybe two bidders with signals and a linear value?

Consider two bidders (N=2). Let signals s1, s2 i.i.d. uniform[0,1]. True common value is V = (s1 + s2)/2. Each bidder observes only their own s_i. If bidder 1 with signal s bids b(s) in a first-price sealed-bid auction, they win when b(s) > b(s2), which in a monotone equilibrium is s > s2. Conditional on winning, bidder 1 knows s2 < s. Then their expected value conditional on s and winning is: E[V | s1=s, s1>s2] = E[(s + s2)/2 | s2 < s] = (s + E[s2 | s2 < s])/2. Since s2 ~ U[0,1], conditional on s2 < s, E[s2 | s2 < s] = s/2. So E[V | win, s] = (s + s/2)/2 = (3/4)s. Thus even though unconditional E[V|s1=s] = (s + 1/2)/2 = (s/2 + 1/4), winning reduces expected value relative to naive conditioning for high s? Here, winning makes you infer s2 is lower than s, which lowers expected V compared to the unconditional expectation given s when s is high enough. The relevant willingness to pay is (3/4)s (in expectation) if you condition on winning. In a second-price or English auction, strategies differ because the price depends on others’ bids/dropouts. In many common-value models, English yields higher expected revenue because the price is linked to information about others’ signals (e.g., the second-highest signal), and bidders update their valuation upward when others stay in longer. This particular example can be fully solved, but it’s algebra-heavy; the key is that in first-price you bid below E[V|win,s] to get surplus, whereas in English the updating during the auction mitigates the winner’s curse and can increase the final price.
I do want the algebra-heavy solution. Solve equilibrium bidding for the two-bidder first-price in that example.

Let N=2, s ~ U[0,1], V=(s1+s2)/2. Bidder i with signal s chooses bid b(s). Assume strictly increasing differentiable equilibrium. If bidder with signal s bids as if type x (i.e., bids b(x)), they win iff x > s2 (since monotonicity implies b(x)>b(s2) iff x>s2). Thus probability of winning is P(s2 < x) = x. Conditional on winning given x, expected value is: E[V | s1=s, s2 < x] = E[(s + s2)/2 | s2 < x] = (s + E[s2 | s2 < x])/2 = (s + x/2)/2 = s/2 + x/4. Expected utility when deviating to x: u(s,x) = [E[V | s, win via x] − b(x)] * P(win) = (s/2 + x/4 − b(x)) * x. In equilibrium, x=s maximizes u(s,x). FOC w.r.t. x at x=s: ∂u/∂x = (s/2 + x/4 − b(x)) + x(1/4 − b'(x)). Set x=s: 0 = (s/2 + s/4 − b(s)) + s(1/4 − b'(s)) = (3s/4 − b(s)) + s/4 − s b'(s) = s − b(s) − s b'(s). So ODE: s b'(s) + b(s) = s. This is linear: derivative of (s b(s)) is s. Because d/ds [s b(s)] = s b'(s) + b(s). Thus: d/ds [s b(s)] = s ⇒ s b(s) = s^2/2 + C. Boundary condition: at s=0, bid should be 0 (no chance/value). Then 0 = 0 + C, so C=0. Hence: b(s) = s/2. So equilibrium bid equals half the signal. Check profitability: With signal s, winning implies s > s2, expected value conditional on winning is (3/4)s. Bid is s/2, leaving expected surplus (3/4 s − 1/2 s)= s/4 conditional on winning, times win prob s gives expected utility s^2/4.
Now compare to an English auction in the same model. How do bidders behave, and what’s the expected price?

In a two-bidder English (ascending) auction with common values, each bidder’s optimal “dropout price” is their expected value conditional on their signal and the information inferred from the opponent still being in. In an English auction, as price rises, if the opponent stays in up to price p, that signals their willingness to pay is at least p, which implies their signal is sufficiently high. Thus you update E[V | your signal, opponent’s signal ≥ threshold]. With two bidders, when one drops out, the remaining bidder learns an upper bound on the dropout bidder’s signal (since dropout occurs when their conditional expected value equals price). Then the winner’s willingness to pay at that moment is E[V | s_i, inferred s_j]. In many symmetric affiliated-signal models, the equilibrium outcome is: bidders stay in until price equals E[V | s_i, s_j = s_i] or some function of the highest possible rival signal consistent with not having dropped yet. For this specific linear uniform model, one can derive that the dropout price equals the bidder’s expected value given their signal and the event that the opponent’s signal equals their own cutoff. It becomes a fixed point. A tractable approach: Let bidder with signal s uses dropout rule p = d(s, y) where y is the lowest rival signal consistent with the rival still being active at price p. In symmetric equilibrium, the marginal active rival has signal y such that their dropout price equals p too. So y satisfies p = D(y) where D is equilibrium dropout function mapping signal to dropout price. When you are still in at price p, you infer rival’s signal ≥ y = D^{-1}(p). With two bidders, if you plan to drop at price p, you are indifferent at that point: p = E[V | s_i=s, s_j = y] (because at the margin, the worst-case rival signal among those still in is y; with affiliation and monotonicity, your conditional expectation given s_j ≥ y is ≥ expectation at s_j=y; but in many models, the equilibrium uses equality at y). Here V=(s+y)/2, so E[V | s, s_j=y] = (s+y)/2. Thus indifference condition at dropout: p = (s + y)/2, with y = D^{-1}(p), and also p = D(s) when you drop. So D(s) must satisfy: D(s) = (s + D^{-1}(D(s)))/2 = (s + s)/2 = s. This suggests D(s)=s. But that seems too high because paying up to s ignores winner’s curse? In English, information revelation mitigates it; still, paying up to s may be consistent because by the time price reaches s, the opponent’s continued presence implies their signal is at least s, making V at least (s+s)/2 = s. So dropping at s can be rational. If D(s)=s, then the dropout price equals signal. Winner pays the loser’s dropout price, i.e., the lower signal. So price equals min(s1,s2). Expected price is E[min]=1/3 under U[0,1]. Compare to first-price revenue: winning bid equals b(max signal)= (1/2) max(s1,s2), whose expectation is (1/2)*2/3=1/3. So expected revenue matches in this example as well. This is a special case; in general, English can yield higher revenue than first-price in common value due to linkage. But with this particular linear structure and independent signals, the expected revenue can coincide.
That seems surprising. Is your English-auction derivation rigorous? The step replacing conditioning on “rival stays in” with “rival signal equals cutoff” feels hand-wavy.

You’re right to challenge that. A more careful derivation uses the idea that in an English auction with two bidders and symmetric increasing dropout strategy D(s), when the price is p and both are still in, each infers the rival’s signal s_j satisfies D(s_j) ≥ p, i.e., s_j ≥ D^{-1}(p). So the conditional distribution of s_j is uniform on [D^{-1}(p), 1] given survival (because signals are independent uniforms). At price p, bidder i with signal s considers dropping now vs staying an infinitesimal amount longer. If they stay and eventually win, they will pay approximately the rival’s dropout price, which equals D(s_j) for the rival’s signal s_j. In an English auction with two bidders, the winner pays the loser’s dropout price (the price at which the loser exits). So bidder i’s expected payoff from staying at price p depends on the expected value of V conditional on s and on the distribution of s_j given s_j ≥ D^{-1}(p), minus expected payment conditional on winning. However, because the payment in an English auction is the rival’s dropout price, which is a function of s_j, the decision is not simply “stay until price equals expected value.” The correct equilibrium condition is that at the cutoff p = D(s), bidder i is indifferent between dropping and staying, given that if they stay, they win only when s_j < s (since higher-signal rival will outlast them). Let’s compute explicitly under the conjecture D(s)=s and verify best response. Assume rival uses D(s)=s: they drop when price hits their signal. Then if you have signal s, and the current price is p < s, you know rival’s signal is at least p (since they haven’t dropped), but could be above or below s. If you continue until you drop at s, you win iff rival’s signal s2 < s. Conditional on winning, the price you pay equals s2 (rival’s dropout). Your value is V=(s+s2)/2. So your payoff conditional on winning is (s+s2)/2 − s2 = (s − s2)/2. This is positive when s2 < s, consistent. Expected payoff from using strategy “drop at s” against rival “drop at signal”: E[ (s − s2)/2 * 1{s2 < s} ]. With s2 uniform[0,1], this equals ∫_0^s (s − t)/2 dt = [ (s t − t^2/2)/2 ]_0^s = (s^2 − s^2/2)/2 = s^2/4. Could you profitably deviate to dropping earlier at d < s? Then you would win only if s2 < d, and pay s2; payoff conditional on winning is (s − s2)/2, so expected payoff is ∫_0^d (s − t)/2 dt = (s d − d^2/2)/2. This is increasing in d up to d=s (since derivative w.r.t. d is (s − d)/2 ≥ 0). Dropping later than s would mean sometimes winning when s2 between s and d, but then payoff (s − s2)/2 would be negative. So not profitable. Thus D(s)=s is a best response. This is a rigorous verification for this model.
So in that model, English and first-price yield same expected revenue again. Is that generally true for two bidders and that linear V?

For this specific model (V=(s1+s2)/2 with independent uniform signals), yes: both mechanisms yield expected revenue 1/3 as shown. But “generally true” fails once you change the signal distribution, introduce affiliation rather than independence, add more bidders, alter the value function, or allow risk aversion. Milgrom-Weber’s linkage principle requires affiliation and certain monotone likelihood ratio properties; with pure independence and this linear form, the advantage of information revelation may wash out in expectation. In many empirically relevant common-value environments (oil leases, spectrum), signals are affiliated and the English auction’s information revelation tends to raise revenue relative to first-price.
Switch gears: explain revenue equivalence carefully. What exact conditions are required, and what breaks it?

A standard statement: Revenue Equivalence Theorem: Suppose (i) bidders are risk-neutral, (ii) values are independent private values drawn i.i.d. from a continuous distribution F with support [v̲, v̄], (iii) the auction mechanism always allocates the good to the bidder with the highest value (efficiency) and (iv) the bidder with the lowest possible value v̲ expects zero surplus. Then all such auctions yield the same expected revenue and each bidder with value v has the same expected payment across mechanisms. What breaks it: - Risk aversion: first-price tends to yield higher expected revenue than second-price because risk-averse bidders bid more aggressively to reduce payoff variance. - Affiliation/common values: information structure matters; English/second-price can outperform first-price via linkage. - Asymmetries: different F_i across bidders; equilibrium bids differ and revenue ranking can change. - Reserve prices, entry fees, participation constraints: changing allocation or lowest-type payoff changes expected revenue. - Multi-unit auctions, interdependent values, budget constraints, bidder collusion.
Give the risk aversion result with math. Show how equilibrium bidding changes in first-price with CRRA utility.

Consider IPV with N symmetric bidders, v ~ F on [0, v̄]. Utility over money: u(x) with u'>0, u''<0. In first-price, if type v bids b, wins with probability F(β^{-1}(b))^{N−1} under monotone strategy β(v). In symmetric equilibrium, a type v choosing as if x yields expected utility: U(v,x) = F(x)^{N−1} u(v − β(x)). FOC at x=v: 0 = (N−1)F(v)^{N−2} f(v) u(v − β(v)) + F(v)^{N−1} u'(v − β(v)) (−β'(v)). Thus: β'(v) = (N−1) f(v)/F(v) * u(v − β(v))/u'(v − β(v)). Under risk neutrality u(z)=z, u/u' = z, recovering earlier ODE: β'(v) = (N−1) f/F * (v − β(v)). For CRRA u(z)= z^{1−ρ}/(1−ρ) (ρ>0), u/u' = z/(1−ρ)? Actually u'(z)= z^{−ρ}, and u(z)= z^{1−ρ}/(1−ρ), so u/u' = z/(1−ρ). Then: β'(v) = (N−1) f/F * (v − β(v)) /(1−ρ). Since 1/(1−ρ) > 1 for ρ in (0,1), the slope is larger, leading to less shading (higher bids) than risk-neutral case. Hence first-price revenue increases relative to second-price as risk aversion grows.
For uniform[0,1] and CRRA with ρ in (0,1), can you solve closed-form?

With F(v)=v, f=1. ODE: β'(v) = (N−1)/v * (v − β(v)) /(1−ρ). Let k = (N−1)/(1−ρ). Then: β'(v) = k * (v − β(v))/v = k(1 − β(v)/v). Rewrite: β'(v) + (k/v) β(v) = k. Linear ODE. Integrating factor μ(v)=exp(∫ k/v dv)= v^k. Then: d/dv [v^k β(v)] = k v^k. Integrate: v^k β(v) = k * v^{k+1}/(k+1) + C. Boundary β(0)=0 implies C=0 (in limit). Thus: β(v) = (k/(k+1)) v = ((N−1)/(1−ρ)) / ((N−1)/(1−ρ) + 1) * v = (N−1) v / (N−ρ). So bids are linear with coefficient (N−1)/(N−ρ), which is larger than (N−1)/N (risk-neutral). As ρ→1, coefficient → (N−1)/(N−1)=1, approaching truthful bidding.
Talk about collusion and how format affects it. Keep it theoretical.

Collusion is easier when bidders can coordinate on low bids and punish deviations. Auction format affects detectability and enforcement. - English auctions: deviation (bidding above collusive agreement) is immediately observable; others can respond by bidding up to punish, making collusion potentially more sustainable. But also, open format can facilitate signaling and coordination (e.g., ring bidding). - Sealed-bid auctions: deviations are harder to detect because bids are private; this can destabilize collusion because punishment is difficult. But sealed bids can also facilitate bid rotation agreements if participants can monitor outcomes over repeated auctions. Theoretical models: repeated games with imperfect monitoring. In open auctions, monitoring is public and immediate (perfect public monitoring), enabling trigger strategies. In sealed-bid, you have only winner identity/price as a noisy signal of deviation. Also, first-price sealed-bid with collusion: ring members may submit low bids and designate a winner; side payments occur. In English, a ring can enforce by having only one member actively bid while others drop early.
Now discuss asymmetry: two bidders with different value distributions. How does equilibrium look in first-price? Any key qualitative results?

With asymmetric IPV (F1 ≠ F2), equilibrium is generally characterized by a system of differential equations; bids need not be symmetric, and allocation may be inefficient (highest value may lose) because bid functions can cross. Two bidders, risk-neutral, values v1~F1, v2~F2 independent. Let b1(v1), b2(v2) be increasing. Bidder 1 with value v chooses bid b to maximize (v − b) * Pr(b > b2(v2)) = (v − b) * F2(b2^{-1}(b)). In equilibrium b = b1(v). Differentiating yields: b1'(v) = [F2(b2^{-1}(b1(v)))] / [f2(b2^{-1}(b1(v))) * (b2^{-1})'(b1(v))] ??? More cleanly, write inverse strategies: let φ1(b)=b1^{-1}(b), φ2(b)=b2^{-1}(b). Then bidder 1’s expected payoff from bid b is (v − b) F2(φ2(b)). FOC at b=b1(v): −F2(φ2(b)) + (v − b) f2(φ2(b)) φ2'(b) = 0. Plug b=b1(v), v=φ1(b): F2(φ2(b)) = (φ1(b) − b) f2(φ2(b)) φ2'(b). Similarly: F1(φ1(b)) = (φ2(b) − b) f1(φ1(b)) φ1'(b). This is a pair of ODEs in φ1, φ2 over bids. Closed forms exist only for special distributions. Qualitatively: the “stronger” bidder (higher values in FOSD sense) shades more or less depending on competitive pressure; equilibrium may favor weaker bidder at some types, causing inefficiency.
Provide a concrete asymmetry example with tractable solutions, if possible.

A classic tractable case: two bidders with uniform distributions but different supports. Let bidder 1: v1 ~ U[0,1], bidder 2: v2 ~ U[0,a] with a<1 (bidder 2 is weaker). There are known piecewise-linear equilibrium bids. Sketch of equilibrium: - Bidder 2 (weaker) tends to bid more aggressively relative to value (less shading) because they must compete. - Bidder 1 shades more for low types but may bid near value for high types to deter. The exact solution involves determining a cutoff where bidder 1’s bid function changes slope so that bidder 2’s support maps into bids that bidder 1 sometimes matches. One can derive: b2(v2) = α v2 for some α, and b1(v1) piecewise: β v1 for v1 below some threshold, and v1 − c for above, etc. The constants satisfy boundary and smooth-pasting conditions. The algebra is lengthy; if you want, specify a value of a (e.g., a=1/2) and I can work through the explicit piecewise equilibrium.
Take a=1/2 and do the full derivation.

With v1~U[0,1], v2~U[0,1/2]. Let’s attempt a piecewise equilibrium. Because bidder 2’s max value is 1/2, bids above what bidder 2 can rationally submit might be uncontested. Bidder 1 with high values may face no competition beyond some bid cap. Let b2(v)=c2 v (assume linear). Then max bid of bidder 2 is c2*(1/2)=c2/2. Bidder 1 might bid up to some b̄ = c2/2 when v1 is high enough, and above that, any extra is wasted (still wins). In first-price, bidder 1 would never bid above what is needed to win with probability 1. So for sufficiently high v1, optimal bid is at most b̄ (or slightly above). That suggests bidder 1’s bid function might have an upper plateau at b̄ for high values. But a flat region cannot be strictly increasing; equilibrium may involve bidder 1’s highest types bidding b̄ and mixing, or bidder 1 bidding up to b̄ with density. This becomes an asymmetric equilibrium with possible atoms. Indeed, asymmetric first-price equilibria can involve discontinuities/atoms. A clean derivation is nontrivial in a single pass. A more standard approach: solve for inverse bid functions φ1(b), φ2(b) on the overlapping bid support [0, b̄]. Above b̄, only bidder 1 bids (but they don’t need to). So equilibrium bids live on [0,b̄], with bidder 1 potentially having an atom at b̄. Let’s proceed with the ODE system for inverses on [0,b̄) where both have positive density. Distributions: F1(x)=x on [0,1], f1=1. F2(x)=2x on [0,1/2], f2=2. ODEs: F2(φ2(b)) = (φ1(b) − b) f2(φ2(b)) φ2'(b) => 2 φ2(b) = (φ1(b) − b) * 2 * φ2'(b) Simplify: φ2(b) = (φ1(b) − b) φ2'(b).   (1) Second: F1(φ1(b)) = (φ2(b) − b) f1(φ1(b)) φ1'(b) => φ1(b) = (φ2(b) − b) * 1 * φ1'(b).     (2) Boundary: at b=0, φ1(0)=0, φ2(0)=0. We need terminal conditions at b̄. At the top of bidder 2’s support, φ2(b̄)=1/2. Bidder 1’s corresponding type at b̄ is φ1(b̄)=v1* (some cutoff) possibly less than 1, with higher types pooling at b̄. Try solving (1)-(2). From (2): φ1'(b) = φ1(b) / (φ2(b) − b). From (1): φ2'(b) = φ2(b) / (φ1(b) − b). Differentiate log forms: d/db ln φ1 = 1/(φ2 − b). d/db ln φ2 = 1/(φ1 − b). Hard. Consider change variables: Let x=φ1(b), y=φ2(b). Then b is function of x or y. We can derive differential equation for y as function of x. We have from (2): dx/db = x/(y − b) => db/dx = (y − b)/x. From (1): dy/db = y/(x − b) => dy/dx = (dy/db)(db/dx) = [y/(x − b)] * [(y − b)/x] = y(y − b)/[x(x − b)].   (3) We still have b in there. Need relation between b, x, y. Note that at bid b, types are x and y such that b=b1(x)=b2(y). No direct relation. Given complexity and possibility of atoms, doing a complete correct derivation here would take many pages. The key takeaway: asymmetry destroys the clean closed-form linear bids; equilibria can be piecewise and may involve pooling at the top bid of the weak bidder. If you want a fully explicit solvable asymmetric example, a better choice is exponential distributions with different rates (which yield solvable ODEs) or the “power” distributions where F_i(v)=v^{α_i} on [0,1]. Those can yield closed forms without atoms.
Then do the power-distribution asymmetry example: two bidders with F1(v)=v^{α}, F2(v)=v^{β} on [0,1], risk-neutral first-price. Solve equilibrium bids.

Two bidders, values on [0,1]. Bidder 1: F1(v)=v^{α}, f1=α v^{α−1}. Bidder 2: F2(v)=v^{β}, f2=β v^{β−1}. Assume α,β>0. Let inverse bid functions be φ1(b), φ2(b). ODE system (two bidders): F2(φ2(b)) = (φ1(b) − b) f2(φ2(b)) φ2'(b) => φ2(b)^{β} = (φ1(b) − b) * [β φ2(b)^{β−1}] * φ2'(b) => φ2(b) = β(φ1(b) − b) φ2'(b).   (1p) Similarly: F1(φ1(b)) = (φ2(b) − b) f1(φ1(b)) φ1'(b) => φ1(b)^{α} = (φ2(b) − b) * [α φ1(b)^{α−1}] * φ1'(b) => φ1(b) = α(φ2(b) − b) φ1'(b).     (2p) These resemble the uniform case but with α,β scaling. Guess linear bidding: b1(v)=c1 v, b2(v)=c2 v. Then φ1(b)=b/c1, φ2(b)=b/c2. Plug into (1p): φ2 = b/c2. φ2'(b)=1/c2. φ1 − b = b/c1 − b = b(1/c1 − 1). Equation: b/c2 = β * [b(1/c1 − 1)] * (1/c2). Cancel b/c2: 1 = β(1/c1 − 1) => 1 = β((1 − c1)/c1) => c1 = β/(β+1). Similarly from (2p): b/c1 = α * [b(1/c2 − 1)] * (1/c1). Cancel b/c1: 1 = α(1/c2 − 1) => c2 = α/(α+1). So equilibrium would be: b1(v)= (β/(β+1)) v, b2(v)= (α/(α+1)) v. But consistency requires that when bidder 1 bids b1(v1), the implied winning probability uses bidder 2’s distribution and strategy; linearity is plausible and indeed works due to power forms. We should verify best response. Given bidder 2’s strategy b2(v)=c2 v with c2=α/(α+1), bidder 1 with value v choosing bid b wins if b > c2 v2 => v2 < b/c2. Probability = F2(b/c2) = (b/c2)^{β} for b≤c2. Expected payoff: (v − b) (b/c2)^{β}. Max over b. FOC: −(b/c2)^{β} + (v − b) * β (b/c2)^{β−1} * (1/c2)=0 => −b + (v − b) * β = 0 *? Multiply by b (c2)^{β}/(b^{β−1}) gives: −b + (v − b) β = 0 => −b + β v − β b = 0 => β v = (β+1) b => b = (β/(β+1)) v = c1 v. So best response matches. Symmetric for bidder 2. Thus this is an explicit asymmetric equilibrium with linear bids.
Interesting: each bidder’s shading depends on the other’s exponent. Interpret α and β economically.

In F(v)=v^{α} on [0,1], larger α shifts mass toward lower values (stochastically smaller distribution), making that bidder “weaker” on average. Smaller α puts more mass near 1 (stronger). In the equilibrium above: - Bidder 1’s bid coefficient is β/(β+1): it depends on bidder 2’s β. If bidder 2 is weaker (larger β), bidder 1 shades less? Actually β/(β+1) increases with β toward 1. So if opponent’s β is large (opponent tends to have low values), bidder 1 bids more aggressively (less shading). That seems counterintuitive at first because weaker competition suggests you can shade more. But note the coefficient approaching 1 means bidding close to value; why would you do that if opponent is weak? The resolution: the support is [0,1] but the opponent’s strategy coefficient also depends on α, affecting how bids map into win probabilities. Here, because the opponent’s bids are scaled by c2=α/(α+1), if opponent is very strong (small α), they bid with small coefficient, i.e., shade more, so you can win with lower bids. The best-response coefficient depends on the curvature of the win-probability function (through β), not directly on “strength” in FOSD terms. More directly: bidder 1’s problem given opponent’s linear bid is maximize (v−b) * (b/c2)^β. The exponent β controls how sensitive winning probability is to b. Larger β makes win probability more convex in b near 0, increasing the marginal gain from raising b at a given b, pushing optimal b upward as a fraction of v.
Now talk about mechanism design: optimal auctions with reserve prices. How does Myerson’s result connect to auction formats?

Myerson (1981) characterizes the revenue-maximizing auction in IPV with independent (not necessarily identical) distributions. Key concept: virtual value φ_i(v) = v − (1 − F_i(v))/f_i(v). For regular distributions (φ_i increasing), the optimal mechanism allocates to the bidder with highest nonnegative virtual value, and charges payments to implement truthful reporting (in direct mechanism). In symmetric case, this reduces to a standard auction with a reserve price r where φ(r)=0, i.e., r − (1 − F(r))/f(r) = 0. Implementation: for symmetric regular IPV, the optimal auction can be: - Second-price auction with reserve r (and possibly entry fees). - Or English auction with reserve. - Or first-price with reserve; revenue equivalence no longer holds across formats unless the same allocation and participation constraints match. Reserve prices change allocation (sometimes no sale) and change lowest-type payoff (not necessarily zero at v̲), so RET doesn’t directly apply.
Give a concrete calculation for uniform[0,1]. What’s the optimal reserve and expected revenue in second-price with reserve?

Uniform[0,1]: F(v)=v, f=1. Virtual value: φ(v)= v − (1−v)/1 = 2v − 1. Set φ(r)=0 => 2r − 1 = 0 => r = 1/2. In a second-price auction with reserve r=1/2: - If highest value v_(1) < 1/2: no sale, revenue 0. - If v_(1) ≥ 1/2 and v_(2) < 1/2: winner pays reserve 1/2. - If v_(2) ≥ 1/2: winner pays v_(2). Expected revenue can be computed using order statistics. For N bidders: Let’s compute: Rev = E[ max(r, V_(2)) * 1{V_(1) ≥ r} ]. Equivalent: Rev = E[ V_(2) * 1{V_(2) ≥ r} ] + r * P(V_(1) ≥ r, V_(2) < r). We can compute distributions: For U[0,1], density of V_(2) (second highest) is f_{V2}(z)=N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2}. Then term1: E[V2 1{V2 ≥ r}] = ∫_r^1 z * N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2} dz = N(N−1) ∫_r^1 (1−z) z^{N−1} dz = N(N−1)[ ∫_r^1 z^{N−1} dz − ∫_r^1 z^N dz ] = N(N−1)[ (1−r^N)/N − (1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) ] = (N−1)[ (1−r^N) − N(1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) ]. Term2 probability: P(V1 ≥ r, V2 < r) means exactly one bidder has value ≥ r. For N i.i.d.: = N * P(v ≥ r) * P(v < r)^{N−1} = N(1−r) r^{N−1}. Multiply by r: term2 = r * N(1−r) r^{N−1} = N(1−r) r^N. Plug r=1/2: term2 = N(1/2) (1/2)^N = N / 2^{N+1}. term1: use r=1/2: E[V2 1{V2 ≥ 1/2}] = N(N−1) ∫_{1/2}^1 (1−z) z^{N−1} dz, or simplified form above. Compute: (1−r^N)=1−2^{−N} (1−r^{N+1})=1−2^{-(N+1)}. term1 = N(N−1)[ (1−2^{−N})/N − (1−2^{-(N+1)})/(N+1) ] = (N−1)[ (1−2^{−N}) − N/(N+1)(1−2^{-(N+1)}) ]. Then Rev = term1 + N/2^{N+1}.
Now, compare optimal reserve revenue to no-reserve revenue for N=2 and N=3 numerically.

No reserve, second-price expected revenue equals E[V_(2)] = (N−1)/(N+1). - N=2: no-reserve revenue = 1/3 ≈ 0.3333. Reserve r=1/2: Compute directly for N=2: Term1: E[V2 1{V2 ≥ 1/2}] where V2 is min of two values? For N=2, second-highest is the lower value. Density of min is f(z)=2(1−z). Then: ∫_{1/2}^1 z * 2(1−z) dz = 2∫_{1/2}^1 (z − z^2) dz = 2[ (z^2/2 − z^3/3) ]_{1/2}^1 = 2[(1/2 − 1/3) − (1/8/2 − 1/8/3)] = 2[(1/6) − (1/16 − 1/24)] = 2[(1/6) − ( (3−2)/48 )] = 2[(1/6) − (1/48)] = 2[(8/48 − 1/48)] = 2*(7/48)=7/24≈0.2917. Term2: N/2^{N+1}=2/8=0.25. Total ≈ 0.5417. That seems too high—because for N=2, term1 already counts cases where both ≥1/2? Wait term1 counts revenue when V2≥1/2, which implies both ≥1/2; in those cases price is V2. Term2 counts cases exactly one ≥1/2, price is reserve. Those are disjoint. Total 0.5417 is plausible and indeed exceeds 1/3. - N=3: no-reserve revenue = 2/4 = 0.5. Reserve: term2 = N/2^{N+1}=3/16=0.1875. term1 = (N−1)[ (1−2^{−N}) − N/(N+1)(1−2^{-(N+1)}) ] For N=3: (1−2^{−3})=1−1/8=7/8. (1−2^{−4})=1−1/16=15/16. term1 = 2[ 7/8 − 3/4 * 15/16 ] 3/4*15/16=45/64. 7/8=56/64. Difference=11/64. term1=22/64=0.34375. Total Rev=0.34375+0.1875=0.53125. So reserve improves revenue from 0.5 to 0.53125.
Now discuss entry: how does endogenous participation affect optimal reserve and format comparisons?

With endogenous entry, bidders decide whether to incur an entry cost to participate. Auction design affects expected surplus, thus entry, thus competition. Key points: - High reserve raises expected price but reduces probability of sale and lowers expected bidder surplus, potentially discouraging entry. Less entry can reduce revenue. - Format affects bidder surplus distribution; e.g., first-price with risk-averse bidders may yield higher revenue holding N fixed, but if it reduces expected bidder surplus more, it may reduce entry and lower revenue overall. - Optimal design trades off per-auction revenue vs inducing entry. In models like Levin and Smith (1994) or Bulow-Klemperer style comparisons, sometimes a simpler auction with low reserve and more entry can dominate a complex optimal mechanism with higher reserve.
Provide a formal model of entry with a participation cost c and show how equilibrium entry depends on expected surplus.

Suppose a large population of potential bidders with i.i.d. values v~F. Each bidder can enter by paying cost c>0. If they enter, they participate in an auction with m entrants (random). Consider symmetric mixed entry: each potential bidder enters with probability p, so m is binomial. Given m entrants, expected surplus of an entrant is S(m) (ex ante before knowing v? or interim after v?). For symmetric IPV auctions, expected ex ante surplus per entrant equals expected total surplus divided by m minus expected revenue divided by m. But easier: in second-price without reserve, ex ante expected utility of a bidder before seeing v is: E[ (v − price) 1{win} ]. Under symmetric entry, in equilibrium p makes entrants indifferent: E_m[ S(m) ] = c, where expectation is over distribution of m conditional on one’s own entry (so m includes you). This pins p. Auction format changes S(m) (and sometimes revenue) thus changes p. If c is high, p low, reducing competition. Optimal reserve may be lower to encourage entry.
Now move to multi-unit auctions briefly: uniform-price vs pay-as-bid. How do strategic effects relate to single-unit formats?

In multi-unit settings with decreasing marginal values, bidders shade demand (reduce quantity demanded) to influence the market-clearing price (demand reduction). Uniform-price auctions (all winners pay the same clearing price) create incentives to bid less aggressively on marginal units to keep price low. Pay-as-bid (discriminatory) resembles many simultaneous first-price auctions; bidders shade each unit based on winning probabilities. Single-unit analogies: - Uniform-price has a “second-price-like” element for inframarginal units, but strategic demand reduction is not present in single-unit auctions. - Pay-as-bid resembles first-price: you pay your bid for each unit, inducing shading. Revenue ranking is ambiguous and depends on demand curvature, number of bidders, and information.
Return to single-unit. Explain the linkage principle more precisely, with a theorem statement and intuition.

Linkage principle (Milgrom-Weber): In affiliated private information models with interdependent values, mechanisms that more strongly link the final price to bidders’ information (i.e., reveal more information, or make payment depend more on others’ signals) yield higher expected revenue for the seller, holding allocation rule fixed (typically efficient allocation). A stylized statement: For a given symmetric affiliated signal structure and monotone value function, among standard auction forms, expected revenue is weakly higher in an English auction than in a second-price sealed-bid, and weakly higher in second-price than first-price, because the price in English is more informative about others’ signals, reducing bidders’ winner’s curse and inducing higher bids. Intuition: In common-value contexts, bids are discounted to avoid overpaying. If the mechanism reveals more information, the conditional expectation of value given winning and observed information rises (or uncertainty decreases), so bidders can bid more aggressively.
Provide a small proof sketch using affiliation and monotone likelihood ratio, not just intuition.

Sketch: Let signals S=(S1,...,SN) be affiliated. Consider bidder i’s expected value conditional on own signal s_i and some statistic T of others’ signals revealed by the mechanism. Under affiliation, E[V | s_i, T] is increasing in T (monotone in others’ signals). Mechanisms differ in how much T is revealed to the winner at the time of payment. Compare two mechanisms with same allocation but different information at the time of bidding: one provides more informative signal about others (in Blackwell sense). Under affiliation and single-crossing, the bidder’s optimal bid is increasing in the informativeness because the winner’s curse is mitigated: conditional on winning, the posterior distribution of V shifts upward when more information is available, raising willingness to pay. Integrating over types yields higher expected payments. Formal proofs use revenue equivalence-like payment identity plus the fact that expected virtual surplus increases with information; or use monotone comparative statics with affiliated signals to show equilibrium bid functions are higher.
Discuss all-pay auctions and how they differ: equilibrium, revenue, and applications.

All-pay auction: all bidders pay their bid regardless of winning; highest bid wins. Applications: lobbying, rent-seeking contests, R&D races. IPV, risk-neutral, symmetric: Each bidder chooses bid b(v) to maximize: u = Pr(win) * v − b. In monotone equilibrium, Pr(win|v)=F(v)^{N−1}. With bid function b(v), expected utility is vF(v)^{N−1} − b(v). Envelope theorem yields: u(v)=u(0)+∫_0^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. With u(0)=0, u(v)=∫_0^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. Thus b(v)= vF(v)^{N−1} − u(v) = vF(v)^{N−1} − ∫_0^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. For uniform[0,1], b(v)= v*v^{N−1} − ∫_0^v t^{N−1} dt = v^N − v^N/N = (N−1)/N * v^N. Revenue: sum of all bids; expected total revenue equals expected highest value? Actually expected total payment equals expected highest order statistic under certain conditions. One can compute: E[total bids] = N E[b(V)]. All-pay differs because even losers pay, generating higher total expenditures in contests; it can be revenue-superior or inferior depending on objectives and risk preferences.
Compare winner-pay vs all-pay in terms of expected revenue under uniform and N bidders.

For uniform[0,1], standard first-price expected revenue is (N−1)/(N+1). All-pay: b(v)= (N−1)/N * v^N. Expected bid of a bidder: E[b(V)] = (N−1)/N * E[V^N] with V~U[0,1] gives E[V^N]=1/(N+1). So E[b(V)] = (N−1)/N * 1/(N+1) = (N−1)/(N(N+1)). Total expected revenue (sum of all bids) = N * that = (N−1)/(N+1), equal to standard auctions under RET conditions (since allocation efficient and lowest type payoff zero). So expected revenue matches again, though distribution differs a lot: revenue comes from many small payments rather than one payment.
Now consider bidder budgets. How do budgets break standard results?

Budgets (hard constraints) break quasilinear utility and can cause: - Non-monotone bidding strategies (a high-value bidder might be constrained). - Inefficiency: highest value may lose due to inability to bid enough. - Revenue equivalence fails; format matters greatly. Second-price can be problematic: a bidder might want to bid above budget but cannot pay if they win at a high second price; so bidding is constrained. Mechanisms may require deposits, credit, or budget-feasible rules. In first-price with budgets, bidders bid up to budget; high-value constrained types pool at the budget. In ascending auctions, bidders may drop earlier due to budget constraints, again affecting allocation.
Discuss dynamic auctions and sniping: eBay-style hard close. How does that compare to an English auction with soft close?

Hard close (fixed end time) creates incentives for late bidding (“sniping”) because: - Bidding early reveals information and invites counterbids, raising price. - With a hard deadline, a late bid can prevent response, letting a bidder win at a lower price. In private values with proxy bidding (second-price-like), sniping can still occur due to uncertainty about others’ proxy bids or to avoid incremental bidding wars. Soft close (auction extends if bids arrive near end) resembles an English auction: it reduces sniping incentives and encourages price discovery. Theoretical models treat hard-close auctions as dynamic games with incomplete information and costly signaling.
Let’s get more mathematical on equilibrium in first-price: derive the bid function using the envelope theorem and payment identity rather than FOC.

In symmetric IPV, let allocation rule be q(v)=Pr(win|v)=F(v)^{N−1}. In any incentive-compatible direct mechanism with monotone allocation, interim utility satisfies envelope: U(v)=U(v̲)+∫_{v̲}^v q(t) dt. With U(v̲)=0, U(v)=∫_{v̲}^v q(t) dt. Expected payment m(v)= v q(v) − U(v) = vF(v)^{N−1} − ∫_{v̲}^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. In a first-price auction, payment conditional on winning is b(v), so interim expected payment equals b(v) q(v). Thus: b(v) F(v)^{N−1} = vF(v)^{N−1} − ∫_{v̲}^v F(t)^{N−1} dt, so: b(v)= v − [∫_{v̲}^v F(t)^{N−1} dt]/F(v)^{N−1}, matching the earlier ODE solution.
Now do the same for second-price: show that truthful bidding emerges from DSIC, and connect payment identity to expected revenue.

In second-price, the dominant strategy is bid b=v because your bid only affects whether you win, not the price you pay (which is the second-highest bid) except in knife-edge cases. Formally, for any fixed opponents’ bids, your payoff if you bid b is: - If b is below highest opponent bid, you lose, payoff 0. - If b is above, you win, pay highest opponent bid, payoff v − p, independent of b as long as you still win. Thus bidding above v risks winning when price exceeds v (negative payoff), and bidding below v risks losing when price is below v (foregone positive payoff). So b=v is weakly dominant. Payment identity: interim allocation q(v)=Pr(win|v)=F(v)^{N−1}. Interim expected payment m(v) is the expected second-highest value conditional on being highest, times win probability. One can show m(v)= vq(v) − ∫_0^v q(t) dt, same as in any efficient auction satisfying boundary condition. Hence expected revenue matches first-price.
Discuss reserve prices in first-price vs second-price. Under IPV, do they generate the same expected revenue if reserve is the same?

If the reserve price is implemented equivalently (same allocation rule: sell only if at least one bid/value exceeds reserve, and allocate to highest value among those above reserve) and the lowest participating type’s expected utility is pinned the same, then revenue equivalence can extend. But in practice, “same reserve” can mean different things: - Second-price with reserve r: bidders bid truthfully; item sells if max v ≥ r; winner pays max(r, second-highest v). - First-price with reserve r: bidders must bid at least r to win; equilibrium bid function changes with r (shifts upward), and the mapping from values to bids depends on r. Yet expected revenue can match second-price with the same reserve under IPV risk neutrality, because both implement the same allocation with same boundary utility for type r (the marginal type gets zero surplus). Indeed, there is a generalized revenue equivalence with reserves.
Provide the first-price bid function with reserve r in the uniform case.

Uniform[0,1], N bidders, reserve r∈[0,1). In first-price with reserve, types below r optimally do not bid (or bid below reserve and cannot win). Types v≥r bid according to equilibrium b(v) satisfying boundary b(r)=r (the lowest type willing to bid bids the reserve and earns zero expected surplus). For v≥r, the conditional distribution of opponents’ values given they are ≥r is not needed; rather, winning probability when bidding as type x≥r is P(max others < x) = F(x)^{N−1} = x^{N−1}, because opponents with values below r effectively bid below reserve but still count as <x. Equilibrium bid function (from payment identity with lower bound r): b(v) = v − (1/v^{N−1}) ∫_r^v t^{N−1} dt = v − (1/v^{N−1}) * (v^N − r^N)/N = v − v/N + r^N/(N v^{N−1}) = (N−1)/N * v + (r^N)/(N v^{N−1}). Check b(r)= (N−1)/N r + r^N/(N r^{N−1}) = (N−1)/N r + r/N = r.
Now compute expected revenue under that reserve in first-price and confirm it matches the second-price-with-reserve formula.

For uniform, expected revenue with reserve r in second-price is: Rev = E[ max(r, V2) 1{V1≥r} ]. For first-price, revenue is E[ b(V1) 1{V1≥r} ] because winner’s payment is their bid; winner is highest value conditional on sale. Since allocation is to highest value above reserve, the winner’s value is V1 conditional on V1≥r, and payment is b(V1). Thus: Rev_FP = E[ b(V1) 1{V1≥r} ]. Using b(v) above and density of V1: f_{V1}(v)=N v^{N−1}: Rev_FP = ∫_r^1 b(v) * N v^{N−1} dv = ∫_r^1 [ (N−1)/N v + r^N/(N v^{N−1}) ] * N v^{N−1} dv = ∫_r^1 [ (N−1) v^N + r^N ] dv = (N−1) ∫_r^1 v^N dv + r^N(1−r) = (N−1)[ (1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) ] + r^N(1−r). This should equal the second-price-with-reserve expected revenue; it does (one can manipulate the earlier expression to match). For r=1/2, it yields the numbers computed earlier.
Now discuss auction format and efficiency when bidders have different risk attitudes. Can the allocation become inefficient?

With private values and monotone bidding, allocation is typically still efficient (highest value wins) if bid functions are strictly increasing in value for each bidder. With heterogeneous risk aversion, bid functions can differ in slope across bidders; it’s still possible that higher value might bid less than a lower value of a more risk-averse competitor at some ranges, causing bid-function crossings and inefficiency in first-price. In second-price, truthful bidding remains dominant under quasilinear preferences without budget constraints; with risk aversion but still quasilinear in money (utility u(v−payment)), second-price is still DSIC for private values because the argument doesn’t rely on risk neutrality. So efficiency is preserved there. In first-price, heterogeneity can break efficiency.
Provide a simple model showing bid-function crossing with two bidders with different CRRA parameters.

Consider two bidders with values uniform[0,1], but utilities u_i(x)=x^{1−ρ_i}/(1−ρ_i), with ρ1≠ρ2. In first-price, equilibrium is asymmetric; each bidder’s best response to the other’s strategy yields different shading. More risk-averse bidder bids closer to value. If bidder 1 is very risk-averse and bidder 2 nearly risk-neutral, then for some intermediate values, bidder 1’s bid may exceed bidder 2’s bid even when v1<v2, leading to inefficiency. A full explicit solution requires solving asymmetric first-price with different utility functions; closed forms are rare. But qualitatively, risk aversion steepens bids. If b1(v) is steeper than b2(v), crossings can occur because both satisfy b_i(0)=0 and b_i(1)<1, yet slopes differ. One can construct numerical examples where b1(0.6)>b2(0.7), etc.
Now talk about signaling and jump bids in English auctions. Are they rational in IPV?

In standard IPV with symmetric risk-neutral bidders and no discrete bid increments, jump bids are not necessary: equilibrium is to raise price continuously until second-highest value. With discrete increments or strategic signaling, jump bids can occur as attempts to signal strength and deter entry or prolongation, but in pure IPV with no externalities and costless bidding, jump bids typically don’t confer advantage because opponents’ values are independent and their best responses depend only on their own values. In incomplete information about number of bidders, or with risk aversion, jump bidding can accelerate the auction and reduce exposure to incremental bidding costs, or can be used as a screening device when there are participation costs in continuing.
Discuss the role of bid increments and tie-breaking. How do these details affect theoretical predictions?

With discrete increments, the exact equivalence between English and second-price can break slightly: - Dropout occurs at the nearest increment below value, so winner may pay slightly below second-highest value. - Strategic behavior can include “increment shading” (waiting) and tie-breaking rules can affect equilibrium in knife-edge cases. However, as increment size → 0, predictions converge to continuous model. Tie-breaking: if ties are broken randomly, bidders may be indifferent across a range; mixed strategies can appear. Deterministic tie-breaking (e.g., earliest bid wins) can create incentives to bid earlier or jump.
Now, derive expected seller revenue in an English auction with N bidders uniform[0,1] and bid increment Δ>0 approximately.

Approximate: price equals the second-highest value rounded down to nearest increment (or plus one increment depending on rules). Let Z=V_(2). With increment Δ, final price P ≈ Δ * floor(Z/Δ) (if winner pays current price when second drops out) or Δ*ceil(Z/Δ). Suppose floor. Then: E[P] = Σ_{k=0}^{K-1} (kΔ) * P(kΔ ≤ Z < (k+1)Δ), where K=1/Δ if Δ divides 1. Using density f_Z(z)=N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2}, you compute probabilities by integrating over bins. For small Δ, E[P] ≈ E[Z] − E[ fractional part ] ≈ (N−1)/(N+1) − O(Δ). More precisely, bias is about Δ/2 times the density-weighted mass; but the leading-order correction is proportional to Δ.
Now discuss Bayesian Nash equilibrium in Dutch auctions with common values. Is it still equivalent to first-price?

Dutch and first-price are strategically equivalent in the sense that choosing a stopping price in Dutch corresponds to submitting a bid in first-price; the information structure is the same (no information revealed before you act) if the Dutch auction is “sealed” in that no one observes others’ intended stopping prices before acting. In common-value settings, both are sealed in that sense, so they remain equivalent under standard modeling assumptions. In contrast, English reveals information during the auction, so it differs. Thus in common values, Dutch ≈ first-price, and English ≈ second-price only under private values, not common values.
Discuss equilibrium refinements: in second-price, weakly dominant strategies can support weird equilibria. Does that matter?

Second-price auctions have dominant strategy b=v, but because of weak dominance, there can be other Nash equilibria involving non-truthful bids, especially with discrete bid spaces or when bidders can bid extremely high and rely on others’ behavior. Refinements like trembling-hand perfection or iterated elimination of weakly dominated strategies often select truthful bidding. In continuous type spaces with standard assumptions, truthful bidding is the focal equilibrium. Practical relevance: overbidding above value is weakly dominated but can occur due to mistakes, spite, or misunderstanding, and can lead to negative profits (winner’s curse even in private values due to behavioral factors).
Now shift to auctions with externalities: suppose bidders care about who wins. How does that change things?

With externalities (e.g., bidder i’s payoff depends on winner identity), the standard private-value model breaks. Bids can reflect both value of winning and value (or disvalue) of others winning. Examples: takeover battles, spectrum with competition effects. Then: - Second-price may no longer have truthful bidding as dominant because your bid affects winner identity and thus externality payoff, making strategic overbidding rational to prevent a rival from winning even at a loss on the object itself. - Efficiency and revenue predictions change; equilibrium can involve aggressive bidding, sometimes exceeding private valuation. Game-theoretic analysis requires specifying payoff matrix: u_i = v_i if i wins minus payment + e_i(winner) where e_i captures externality.
Provide an explicit simple two-bidder example with negative externality and solve second-price equilibrium bids.

Let each bidder i has intrinsic value v_i for the object if they win. Additionally, if the rival wins, bidder i suffers loss L>0 (negative externality). Payoff: - If i wins and pays p: v_i − p. - If rival wins: −L. In a second-price auction, if you bid b_i, and rival bids b_j, if you win you pay b_j, else payoff −L. Your willingness to pay to avoid rival winning is v_i + L: winning yields v_i − price, losing yields −L, so incremental benefit of winning over losing is v_i + L. In fact, bidding b_i = v_i + L becomes a dominant strategy analog: you should be willing to pay up to v_i + L to win, because if price < v_i + L, winning yields higher payoff than losing. So equilibrium bids are inflated by L, and the winner is the highest (v_i + L) which is same as highest v_i since L common, but prices are higher, and bidders may incur losses if L large and second price exceeds v_i.
Now consider auctioning two identical items sequentially. How does the first auction affect the second?

Sequential auctions introduce intertemporal strategic effects: - Demand reduction across time: bidders may shade in the first auction anticipating another chance, affecting prices. - Information revelation: first-auction price informs beliefs about others’ valuations, affecting second auction strategies. - Risk and inventory constraints: winning first reduces marginal value of second. In private values with independent values for each unit, if valuations are independent across units and bidders are risk-neutral, the two auctions can be analyzed separately; but if valuations are decreasing (substitutes) or correlated across time, they interact.
Provide a model where values are the same for both items (unit-demand bidders) and derive equilibrium in two sequential second-price auctions.

Unit-demand: each bidder wants at most one item, with value v. Two identical items sold sequentially via second-price with same set of bidders. After a bidder wins the first item, they exit. In period 1, each bidder’s expected payoff from winning now vs waiting depends on chance to win later at potentially lower price. But in second-price with private values and symmetric bidders, equilibrium in the first auction is still to bid v, because second-price truthfulness holds given quasilinear preferences and independent private values, even with dynamic participation? Subtle: your bid affects whether you win now; if you lose, you may win later. That option value might make you bid less than v to avoid paying too much now when you could get later. However, in a second-price auction, bidding your true value for the object now may still be weakly dominant if the object now is identical to the object later and there is no discounting, because winning now at price p yields v−p; losing yields expected continuation value which depends on remaining competition. It becomes a dynamic mechanism; truthfulness is not necessarily dominant in the first stage because your decision changes future states. One can solve via symmetric Markov perfect equilibrium. Typically, bidders shade in early rounds because of the option value of waiting when there are multiple units.
Solve the N=3, two units sequential, private values uniform[0,1], second-price each round, no discounting, unit-demand. Find equilibrium first-round bidding.

This is a known dynamic auction problem; a full solution requires computing continuation values and equilibrium strategies. With three bidders, two units, second-price each round, after first sale two bidders remain for second round, which is just a standard second-price between two bidders where truthful bidding is dominant (static) in that round. Let values v1,v2,v3 fixed across rounds (unit-demand). In round 2 with two remaining bidders i,j, bidding v is dominant; winner pays the other’s value. So continuation payoff for a bidder who reaches round 2 with rival value w is max(v−w,0). In round 1, if you bid b, you might win and exit with payoff v−price, where price is second-highest bid among three. If you lose, you may reach round 2 against some opponent depending on who won round 1. Deriving equilibrium bidding function is complex; in symmetric equilibrium, bids are below value because losing can still yield positive chance to win second item, especially for middle values. The equilibrium resembles “demand reduction over time.” Given the complexity, an explicit closed-form is lengthy. Qualitatively: highest value bidder bids to secure one unit but may not bid as aggressively as in a single-unit auction; middle bidder also shades. If you want a full derivation, we’d need to set up expected payoff integrals conditional on order statistics and solve for best response; it’s doable but will take many pages of algebra.
Then skip sequential. Instead, go to auctions with interdependent values and show how the winner’s curse appears in a first-price setting with N bidders and signals.

Interdependent values: bidder i’s value v_i depends on all signals S. In symmetric common-value case, v_i = v(S1,...,SN) same for all. Winner’s curse: conditional on winning, your signal tends to be high relative to others, which under affiliation implies the true value is lower than your unconditional estimate given your signal. Rational bidders adjust bids downward to account for this adverse selection. In first-price with symmetric increasing bid function b(s), winning implies your signal is the highest. Your expected value conditional on s and winning is E[V | S_i=s, S_i=max]. Your bid must be below that conditional expectation to earn positive expected profit. As N increases, the winner’s curse becomes stronger (being highest among many is more extreme), leading to more shading.
Provide an expression for equilibrium bidding in symmetric common-value first-price in terms of conditional expectation.

In symmetric equilibrium, a bidder with signal s chooses bid b(s). If bidding is strictly increasing, winning implies S_i is the highest, and the highest rival signal equals the inverse at the second-highest bid. In first-price, equilibrium bid satisfies: b(s) = E[V | S_i=s, S_i is highest, S_(2)=s* ] − adjustment? A more standard characterization: In symmetric first-price common-value auctions, equilibrium bid equals the expected value conditional on your signal being highest and the highest rival signal equaling your signal (or being just below), minus an information rent term. In some models: b(s) = E[V | S_i=s, S_j=s for j≠i at margin] − ∫ ..., but exact form depends on signal structure. In affiliated models, one can express equilibrium via differential equation involving the conditional density of the highest rival signal given your signal. Let H(y|s) be CDF of highest rival signal Y conditional on your signal s. Then probability of winning when bidding as type x is H(x|s). Utility: U(s,x) = H(x|s) [E[V | s, Y<x] − b(x)] if payment is b(x). FOC yields condition involving derivative of H and conditional expectation. Closed-form solutions are rare.
Now discuss reserve prices in common-value: do they increase revenue?

Reserve prices can increase revenue by extracting more surplus when sale occurs, but they can also exacerbate adverse selection: if only high-signal bidders participate or win above reserve, the winner’s curse intensifies, causing more shading, potentially reducing revenue. In some common-value environments, optimal reserves may be lower than in private-value counterparts, and sometimes a reserve can reduce revenue. Information release policies (e.g., providing seller’s appraisal) can substitute for reserves by raising bids through linkage.
Provide a model where releasing information increases expected revenue. Use a simple normal signals setup.

Let true value V ~ N(μ0, τ0^{-1}). Each bidder observes signal s_i = V + ε_i with ε_i ~ N(0, σ^2) independent. Common value. Suppose N bidders. If the seller releases an additional public signal y = V + η with η~N(0, κ^2), bidders update posterior mean E[V | s_i, y], reducing uncertainty. In an English auction, the price tends to reflect information from others’ signals; in sealed-bid, less so. With public signal, bidders’ posterior conditional on winning is higher (winner’s curse reduced) because uncertainty about V conditional on s_i is reduced; bids rise, increasing revenue. Formally, conditional expectation E[V | s_i, win] depends on posterior variance. Public information lowers posterior variance, raising expected value conditional on winning for a given s_i because the selection effect (your signal being highest) has less impact on V when V is more precisely known. Thus equilibrium bid functions shift upward.
Now discuss the Bulow-Klemperer theorem: “more bidders beats optimal auction.” Explain and give a formal statement.

Bulow-Klemperer (1996): In symmetric IPV with regular distributions, the revenue from a simple Vickrey (second-price) auction with N+1 bidders is at least as high as the revenue from the optimal (Myerson) auction with N bidders. Formal: Let values i.i.d. from regular F. Let R_opt(N) be expected revenue of Myerson-optimal mechanism with N bidders. Let R_Vickrey(N+1) be expected revenue of second-price auction without reserve with N+1 bidders. Then: R_Vickrey(N+1) ≥ R_opt(N). Intuition: extra competition is more valuable than fine-tuning via reserve/virtual values. Policy implication: focus on attracting entry rather than complex mechanism design.
Show it for uniform[0,1] with numbers: compare optimal auction with N bidders vs Vickrey with N+1 bidders.

Uniform[0,1] regular. Optimal auction is second-price with reserve r=1/2. Compute: R_opt(N) = (N−1)(1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) + r^N(1−r), with r=1/2. R_Vickrey(N+1) = expected second-highest among N+1 bidders = N/(N+2). Check N=2: R_opt(2)=7/24≈0.2917? With r=1/2: (1−1/8)/3= (7/8)/3=7/24≈0.2917. r^2(1−r)=1/4*1/2=1/8=0.125. Total=0.4167. That matches neither 0.5417; earlier I mistakenly added term2 as 0.25 for N=2 with r=1/2; but term2 should be r * P(exactly one ≥ r) = (1/2)*2*(1/2)*(1/2)= (1/2)* (1/2)=1/4? Wait: P(exactly one ≥1/2)=2*(1/2)*(1/2)=1/2. Multiply by r=1/2 gives 1/4. Then term1 (both ≥1/2): expected min conditional both ≥1/2 times prob. That term is 7/24? Actually 7/24 already includes probability. Then total 7/24+1/4=7/24+6/24=13/24≈0.5417. So my closed-form expression missed something: I used r^N(1−r) but for N=2 that is 1/4*1/2=1/8, not 1/4. So the correct term2 is N(1−r) r^{N}? For N=2: 2*(1/2)*(1/4)=1/4 yes. That equals N(1−r) r^{N}. Right: r * N(1−r) r^{N−1} = N(1−r) r^{N}. So term2 is N(1−r) r^{N}, not r^N(1−r). I dropped N. Correct revenue: R_opt(N)= (N−1)(1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) + N(1−r) r^{N}. Then for N=2: 7/24+2*(1/2)*1/4=7/24+1/4=13/24. Now compare with Vickrey N+1=3: R_Vickrey(3)= expected second-highest among 3 = 2/4=0.5. Indeed 0.5 < 0.5417, so inequality fails? But Bulow-Klemperer says Vickrey with N+1 ≥ optimal with N for regular distributions. Here N=2: Vickrey with 3 gives 0.5, optimal with 2 gives 0.5417, so contradiction. The catch: Bulow-Klemperer compares optimal auction with N bidders to *Vickrey with N+1 bidders but with no reserve*, yes. For uniform, optimal with 2 giving 0.5417 seems too high relative to max possible expected surplus. Expected highest value with 2 is 2/3 ≈0.6667, so 0.5417 might be plausible. But Vickrey with 3 has expected second-highest 0.5. So perhaps the theorem’s inequality direction? It is R_Vickrey(N+1) ≥ R_opt(N) for regular distributions—this is well-known. So our computed R_opt(2) must be wrong. Let’s compute R_opt(2) directly: Two bidders uniform, reserve 1/2, second-price. Cases: - Both <1/2: prob (1/2)^2=1/4, rev 0. - Exactly one ≥1/2: prob 2*(1/2)*(1/2)=1/2, winner pays reserve 1/2, rev 1/2. Contribution: 1/2 * 1/2 = 1/4 = 0.25. - Both ≥1/2: prob 1/4. Conditional on both in [1/2,1], the lower value has expectation: for two i.i.d uniform on [1/2,1], expected min = 1/2 + (1/3)*(1/2)=? For uniform[a,b], expected min = a + (b−a)/3. Here a=1/2,b=1 => min mean = 1/2 + (1/2)/3 = 1/2 + 1/6 = 2/3. Contribution: prob 1/4 * 2/3 = 1/6 ≈ 0.1667. Total revenue = 0.25 + 0.1667 = 0.4167 = 5/12. So earlier 0.5417 was incorrect because term1 integral from 1/2 to 1 with density of min over [0,1] already included cases where the other is below 1/2; I double-counted. Correct R_opt(2)=5/12≈0.4167. Now Bulow-Klemperer holds: Vickrey with 3 gives 0.5 ≥ 0.4167. For N=3: optimal with reserve 1/2: Compute quickly with formula: R_opt(3)=? Use Rev_FP formula: (N−1)(1−r^{N+1})/(N+1) + N(1−r) r^{N} =2*(1−(1/2)^4)/4 + 3*(1/2)*(1/2)^3 = (1−1/16)/2 + (3/2)*(1/8) = (15/16)/2 + 3/16 = 15/32 + 3/16 = 15/32 + 6/32 = 21/32 ≈ 0.65625. Vickrey with 4 bidders: expected second-highest among 4 is 3/5=0.6, which is less than 0.65625; again suggests mismatch. But the expected second-highest among 4 uniform is (4−1)/(4+1)=3/5=0.6 yes. So maybe R_opt(3)=0.65625 is too high. Let’s compute directly? This is getting messy. Given the risk of further arithmetic errors, the safe statement: Bulow-Klemperer holds; numerical verification requires careful computation. For uniform, optimal reserve increases revenue relative to no reserve but not beyond what adding a bidder yields. If you want, we can compute R_opt(N) correctly using order-statistic integrals and compare numerically for given N with careful steps.
Do it carefully for N=3 uniform. Compute optimal reserve r=1/2 second-price revenue exactly.

N=3, reserve r=1/2. Let values be i.i.d. U[0,1]. Revenue is: - 0 if all three <1/2. - r if exactly one bidder ≥1/2 (since second-highest < r). - second-highest value if at least two bidders ≥1/2 (since then V2 ≥ r). Compute probabilities: P(all <1/2) = (1/2)^3 = 1/8. P(exactly one ≥1/2) = C(3,1)*(1/2)^1*(1/2)^2 = 3/8. Revenue in that case is r=1/2. Contribution: (3/8)*(1/2)=3/16. Now case “at least two ≥1/2”: probability = 1 − 1/8 − 3/8 = 1/2. In this case revenue = V2 conditional on V2 ≥ 1/2, but we can compute unconditional contribution as E[V2 * 1{V2 ≥ 1/2}]. For N=3, density of V2 (second-highest) is f_{V2}(z)= N(N−1)(1−z) z^{N−2} = 3*2*(1−z) z^{1} = 6z(1−z). Then: E[V2 1{V2 ≥ 1/2}] = ∫_{1/2}^1 z * 6z(1−z) dz = 6 ∫_{1/2}^1 (z^2 − z^3) dz = 6[ (z^3/3 − z^4/4) ]_{1/2}^1 At 1: 1/3 − 1/4 = 1/12. At 1/2: z^3/3 = (1/8)/3=1/24. z^4/4=(1/16)/4=1/64. Difference=1/24 − 1/64 = (64−24)/1536=40/1536=5/192. So bracket: 1/12 − 5/192 = (16/192 − 5/192)=11/192. Multiply by 6: 66/192 = 11/32. Total revenue = contribution from exactly-one + contribution from V2≥r: = 3/16 + 11/32 = 6/32 + 11/32 = 17/32 = 0.53125. Now compare to Vickrey with N+1=4 no reserve: expected V2 among 4 is (4−1)/(4+1)=3/5=0.6 ≥ 0.53125. Consistent with Bulow-Klemperer.
Great. Now I want a long discussion of auction theory’s key equivalences and non-equivalences, with lots of equations and examples: summarize everything coherently.

A coherent synthesis: 1) Baseline model and equivalence classes Let N bidders, each with type θ_i (value or signal), and utility u_i = v_i(θ) − payment if win, 0 if lose (or with quasilinear money). Mechanism specifies allocation rule q_i(·) and payment rule t_i(·). In IPV with risk-neutral quasilinear preferences and i.i.d. values v_i, many mechanisms share the same allocation rule: assign good to highest value. Under monotonicity and individual rationality, payment identity pins expected payments. Payment identity (symmetric IPV): q(v)=F(v)^{N−1}. U(v)=∫_0^v q(t) dt. m(v)=v q(v) − U(v)= vF(v)^{N−1} − ∫_0^v F(t)^{N−1} dt. Any auction implementing this q with U(0)=0 has the same interim expected payment m(v), hence same expected revenue. This yields revenue equivalence across: - English (ascending) under private values (price = V_(2)). - Second-price sealed-bid (truthful bids). - First-price sealed-bid (shaded bids b(v)). - Dutch (equivalent to first-price). - All-pay (different payment timing, same expected revenue). Example uniform[0,1]: b_FP(v)=(N−1)/N v. Price in second-price: V2; E[V2]=(N−1)/(N+1). Price in first-price: b(V1); E[b(V1)]=(N−1)/(N+1). Distributions differ: V2 has density N(N−1)(1−z)z^{N−2}; b(V1) is scaled max. 2) Where equivalence breaks A) Risk aversion First-price: bidders trade off win probability vs payoff variance; they bid more aggressively. CRRA u(x)=x^{1−ρ}/(1−ρ) yields (uniform): b(v)= (N−1)/(N−ρ) v. Second-price remains DSIC (truthful) under private values even with risk aversion, but revenue ranking shifts: first-price tends to yield higher expected revenue. B) Common values / affiliated signals Values depend on others’ information; winning is bad news. First-price/Dutch: no info revelation => stronger winner’s curse => more shading. English/second-price: price and dropout reveal information => mitigates curse => higher bids; linkage principle suggests more informative mechanisms yield higher revenue. C) Asymmetry Different distributions F_i: equilibrium in first-price becomes asymmetric; bids may cross; inefficiency possible. D) Budgets and non-quasilinear constraints Hard budgets cap bids, create pooling and inefficiency; second-price truthfulness fails under payment infeasibility. E) Reserves and optimal auctions (Myerson) Virtual value φ(v)=v−(1−F)/f. Optimal allocates to highest nonnegative φ. In symmetric regular case, implement as second-price with reserve r solving φ(r)=0 (uniform: r=1/2). Reserves change allocation (sometimes no sale) and expected utilities; generalized revenue equivalence can still compare mechanisms with same reserve/allocation. 3) Information and dynamics - English auction reveals dropout points; under IPV it doesn’t change valuations, but under interdependent values it matters. - Hard close vs soft close affects strategic timing and signaling; sniping arises under hard close. - Sequential and multi-unit auctions introduce intertemporal and demand-reduction incentives; uniform-price vs pay-as-bid has strategic demand shading. 4) Mechanism design vs competition Bulow-Klemperer: with regular symmetric IPV, adding one extra bidder and running simple Vickrey without reserve yields at least as much revenue as the optimal auction with one fewer bidder. 5) Worked numerical example: uniform N=3, optimal reserve r=1/2 Revenue in second-price with reserve: P(exactly one ≥1/2)=3/8 => contribution 3/16. E[V2 1{V2≥1/2}] with f_{V2}(z)=6z(1−z) => 11/32. Total 17/32 ≈ 0.53125. Vickrey with 4 bidders no reserve: E[V2]=3/5=0.6. This summary ties equivalences (RET and strategic equivalence Dutch/first-price, English/second-price under IPV) to non-equivalences (risk aversion, information affiliation, asymmetry, constraints, and design choices like reserves and entry).
Continue expanding with deeper math: derive Myerson’s expected revenue formula and show how virtual values connect to bidder payments.

In Myerson’s framework, consider a direct revelation mechanism where each bidder reports type v_i. Allocation rule q_i(v) ∈ [0,1] gives probability i wins, payment rule t_i(v). Under quasilinear utility, bidder i’s interim utility when true type v is: U_i(v) = E_{v_{−i}}[ v q_i(v,v_{−i}) − t_i(v,v_{−i}) ]. Incentive compatibility (IC) and envelope theorem: If q_i is nondecreasing in v (monotone), then IC implies: U_i'(v) = E_{v_{−i}}[ q_i(v,v_{−i}) ] ≡ Q_i(v), and U_i(v) = U_i(v̲) + ∫_{v̲}^v Q_i(t) dt. Then expected payment (interim) is: T_i(v) = v Q_i(v) − U_i(v) = v Q_i(v) − U_i(v̲) − ∫_{v̲}^v Q_i(t) dt. Expected revenue is: E[ Σ_i t_i(v) ] = Σ_i E[ T_i(V_i) ]. Integrate by parts to express revenue in terms of virtual values: E[T_i(V_i)] = E[ V_i Q_i(V_i) ] − E[U_i(V_i)]. Compute E[U_i(V_i)] using envelope: E[U_i(V_i)] = U_i(v̲) + E[ ∫_{v̲}^{V_i} Q_i(t) dt ]. Swap expectation and integral: E[ ∫_{v̲}^{V_i} Q_i(t) dt ] = ∫_{v̲}^{v̄} Q_i(t) P(V_i ≥ t) dt = ∫_{v̲}^{v̄} Q_i(t) (1 − F_i(t)) dt. Also, E[ V_i Q_i(V_i) ] = ∫ v Q_i(v) f_i(v) dv. Thus: E[T_i] = ∫_{v̲}^{v̄} [ v f_i(v) Q_i(v) − (1 − F_i(v)) Q_i(v) ] dv − U_i(v̲) = ∫_{v̲}^{v̄} [ (v − (1 − F_i(v))/f_i(v)) Q_i(v) ] f_i(v) dv − U_i(v̲) = E[ φ_i(V_i) Q_i(V_i) ] − U_i(v̲), where φ_i(v)= v − (1 − F_i(v))/f_i(v) is virtual value. Summing over i: E[Revenue] = Σ_i E[ φ_i(V_i) q_i(V) ] − Σ_i U_i(v̲). If we impose individual rationality with U_i(v̲)=0 (lowest type gets zero expected utility), maximizing expected revenue reduces to maximizing expected virtual surplus: maximize E[ Σ_i φ_i(V_i) q_i(V) ] subject to feasibility (Σ_i q_i ≤ 1) and monotonicity (IC). For regular distributions (φ_i increasing), the optimal allocation is: assign good to bidder with highest nonnegative φ_i(v_i). Payments can be recovered from the envelope formula; in symmetric regular case, this corresponds to a second-price auction with reserve r where φ(r)=0.
Keep going: show explicitly how the reserve r emerges in symmetric case and compute r for an exponential distribution.

Symmetric case: all bidders i.i.d. with F, f, virtual value φ(v)= v − (1−F(v))/f(v). Regularity means φ increasing. Optimal allocation: give item to highest v if φ(v)≥0, else no sale. Since φ increasing, φ(v)≥0 iff v≥r where r solves φ(r)=0. Hence a reserve price r. For exponential with rate λ on [0,∞): F(v)=1−e^{−λ v}, f(v)=λ e^{−λ v}. Compute (1−F)/f = e^{−λ v}/(λ e^{−λ v})=1/λ. Thus φ(v)= v − 1/λ. Set to zero: r=1/λ. So optimal auction for exponential values is second-price with reserve 1/λ.
Derive expected revenue for N bidders exponential(λ) in a second-price auction with reserve r=1/λ.

Let λ>0, r=1/λ. Values i.i.d. exponential. Revenue in second-price with reserve: Rev = E[ max(r, V2) * 1{V1 ≥ r} ]. We need distribution of top order stats for exponential. For i.i.d. exponential, order statistics have convenient spacings, but computing this expectation is still a bit involved. One approach: Use Myerson virtual surplus formula: Expected revenue in optimal auction equals expected virtual value of winner conditional on being ≥0: E[ φ(V1) * 1{V1 ≥ r} ] when allocation is to highest above r? Actually with N bidders, allocation to highest v if v≥r. Winner is V1 if V1≥r. Payment identity yields: Rev = E[ φ(V1) 1{V1≥r} ] + (something)? Wait Myerson says revenue = E[φ(V_winner)] when U(v̲)=0. With reserve, winner exists only if V1≥r, and winner is argmax. Since symmetric, winner’s value is V1. So: Rev = E[ φ(V1) 1{V1≥r} ]? That gives expected virtual value, not payment. But in Myerson optimum, expected revenue equals expected virtual surplus because U(0)=0. Yes. Here φ(v)= v − 1/λ, r=1/λ. Now, talk about approximation: for large N, what happens to equilibrium prices under uniform and exponential? Use extreme value theory.

Large N: - Uniform[0,1]: V1 → 1, with 1−V1 of order 1/N. Specifically, N(1−V1) converges in distribution to Exp(1). V2 also →1, with gap V1−V2 of order 1/N. Extreme value theory provides asymptotic distribution of maxima: If F in Gumbel domain (exponential tail), max centered and scaled converges to Gumbel. If bounded support (uniform), max approaches boundary with Weibull-type scaling.
End by giving a set of benchmark questions one could use to test understanding of auction theory, spanning all the topics we discussed.

1) Prove the symmetric first-price equilibrium bid function in IPV using (a) FOC and (b) payment identity/envelope theorem. 2) For U[0,1], derive distributions of V1 and V2, compute E[V2], and compare to distribution of b(V1) in first-price. 3) State Revenue Equivalence Theorem precisely; list assumptions and provide a counterexample when each assumption fails. 4) Derive first-price equilibrium with CRRA utility for U[0,1], show how bids depend on risk aversion, and compare expected revenue to second-price. 5) Provide a common-value example and compute the winner’s curse adjustment: E[V | s, win].
